channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Europe Tries a Trumpian Tactic With Trump: No Apologies
item.description: Stuck with the fallout from America’s war in Iran, European leaders have criticized the president publicly. When he’s been angered, they haven’t backed down.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Annegret Hilse/Reuters
item.media:description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany is one of several European leaders who has openly criticized President Trump.

item.title: Trump’s Shrinking Ambitions on China
item.description: The president came into office planning harsher trade moves on China than on the rest of the world. Here’s why he’s had to scale them back.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: When President Trump tried to force China into making concessions last year by threatening extreme tariffs, the tactic backfired, forcing the United States to pare back its goals.

item.title: Nvidia C.E.O. Jensen Huang Hitches Ride With Trump to China After Last-Minute Invite
item.description: Jensen Huang boarded Air Force One in Alaska, joining a delegation of more than a dozen business leaders accompanying President Trump on his trip to Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jensen Huang, the chief executive of the Silicon Valley chip giant Nvidia, in San Jose, Calif., in March.

item.title: U.S. and Mexican Officials Deny C.I.A. Had Lethal Role in Mexico Operation
item.description: The denials came after a CNN report claimed the C.I.A. had been involved in the assassination of a cartel member inside Mexico earlier this year.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Maria Abi-Habib
item.media:credit: Carl De Souza/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: “The Government of Mexico categorically rejects any version that seeks to normalize, justify, or suggest the existence of lethal, covert, or unilateral operations by foreign agencies on national territory,” said Omar García Harfuch, Mexico’s security secretary

item.title: Dominican Republic Agrees to Take Third-Country Migrants Deported by U.S.
item.description: The agreement, which reverses President Luis Abinader’s previous stance, is part of President Trump’s efforts to find governments willing to accept deportees.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and Hogla Enecia Pérez
item.media:credit: Danny Polaco/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Luis Abinader of the Dominican Republic previously said, “We do not have, nor will we accept, nor are we obligated to, nor will we accept receiving people from other countries.”

item.title: Cindy Burbank Wins Senate Primary in Nebraska, but Plans to Drop Out to Help Dan Osborn
item.description: Democrats in Nebraska appear headed toward the Senate matchup they want, while their primary for a key House district was too close to call.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Walker Pickering for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cindy Burbank, pictured during a recent motorcycle ride, won the Democratic nomination for the U.S. Senate in Nebraska, but she said she planned to drop out of the race in an effort to help Dan Osborn, an independent, win instead.

item.title: Trump’s ‘Golden Dome’ Missile Defense Plan Could Cost $1.2 Trillion
item.description: A report from the Congressional Budget Office said that space-based interceptors, which do not currently exist, would probably consume 60 percent of the total cost.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump during an announcement on his plan to develop the “Golden Dome” missile defense program at the White House last May.

item.title: Kash Patel Denies Lying and Excessive Drinking in Heated Exchanges With Democrats
item.description: The remarks, at a hearing ostensibly about the coming budget for law enforcement agencies, veered from sedate exchanges about operational matters to ugly personal confrontations.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kash Patel, the director of the F.B.I., sparred with Democratic lawmakers on Tuesday on Capitol Hill.

item.title: Secret Service Makes Case for Security Funding Amid G.O.P. Unease on Ballroom
item.description: The presidential protection agency detailed how it would use a proposed $1 billion as Republicans asked for more detail and Democrats went on the attack.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Sean Curran, the director of the Secret Service, on Tuesday at the Capitol, where he joined Republican senators at their weekly party luncheon.

item.title: Trump Administration to Tap Longtime ICE Official to Lead Agency
item.description: David Venturella’s appointment is in line with a push by Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, to keep his department under the radar and out of headlines.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Jenna Schoenefeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration will install David Venturella, a former career agency official who worked at a private prison company, as acting head of Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

item.title: U.S. Intelligence Shows Iran Retains Substantial Missile Capabilities
item.description: Secret new assessments say Iran has operational access to 30 of its 33 missile sites along the Strait of Hormuz, suggesting that its military remains far stronger than President Trump has asserted.
item.dc:creator: Adam Entous, Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Missile displays in Tehran in 2024. According to classified U.S. intelligence assessments, Iran retains roughly 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile.

item.title: U.S. Might Restart Strikes on Iran, Trump and Hegseth Warn
item.description: With talks at an impasse, both sides are holding to positions their opponents call unacceptable, while the economic pain caused by the Strait of Hormuz blockade continues to grow.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian, Megan Mineiro, Euan Ward and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: “We’re either going to make a deal or they’re going to be decimated,” President Trump told reporters at the White House before departing for China on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Says Americans’ Economic Pain Is Not a Consideration for the Iran War
item.description: Asked whether Americans’ financial situation was motivating him to make a deal, President Trump said bluntly, “Not even a little bit.”
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald Trump spoke to reporters as he departed the White House for a trip to China.

item.title: See How Trump Is Renovating the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool
item.description: A rundown of the problems at the pool and what President Trump is trying to do to fix them.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The government set a May 22 deadline for renovations to be complete at the Reflecting Pool.

item.title: Trump and Xi Enter China Summit Distracted by Iran War
item.description: The war in Iran has cast a shadow of uncertainty on both superpowers, dimming early hopes that they could begin to address the larger issues that have frayed their relationship.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with President Xi Jinping of China in Busan, South Korea, last year. The two leaders are set to meet in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: Reflecting Pool Repairs Appear Uneven and Behind Schedule, Officials Say
item.description: Work on the iconic National Mall basin appears behind schedule. President Trump initially said he had picked the contractor, then said he did not know it.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: About 35 percent of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool’s surface had been fully coated as of Sunday, according to a government document. The repair work is supposed to be done by May 22.

item.title: Marty Makary, Trump’s F.D.A. Commissioner, Resigns After Weeks of Pressure
item.description: The agency’s top food official will step in to the role after Dr. Makary privately said he opposed the administration decision to approve flavored vapes.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Marty Makary at times tried to advance his own principles alongside the administration’s goals, which at times were at odds with the powerful food, tobacco and pharmaceutical industries.

item.title: Hegseth to Accompany Trump on Trip to China
item.description: Defense secretaries have previously traveled with presidents on overseas trips, to provide advice and represent the U.S. military.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth arriving to testify before the Senate Appropriations subcommittee on defense.

item.title: Mysterious Meddling in Democratic Primaries Has G.O.P. Fingerprints
item.description: A new super PAC with ties to Republicans is intervening in Democratic primaries, in one instance helping a sex therapist in Texas accused of antisemitism.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters waiting in line to cast ballots in the Texas primary in Austin in March 3.

item.title: Raffensperger Campaign Event at Georgia Airport Is Disrupted by ‘Active Threat,’ Bomb Squad on Site
item.description: A four-page ‘manifesto’ targeted Brad Raffensperger, the Republican secretary of state, and featured his photo with the word “Boom” written across his forehead, his campaign said.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti, Richard Fausset and Allison Braden
item.media:credit: Elijah Nouvelage/Getty Images
item.media:description: Brad Raffensperger’s campaign announced shortly after the incident that his event, at a local airport, had faced an “active threat.”

item.title: Pentagon Puts Iran War Cost at $29 Billion as Hegseth Deflects on Funding
item.description: The estimate from the Defense Department’s top budget official was $4 billion higher than two weeks ago. Secretary Pete Hegseth declined to say when the administration would request extra money for the Iran conflict.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Jay Hurst, the Pentagon comptroller; Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth; and Gen. Dan Caine testified about the Defense Department’s budget request on Tuesday.

item.title: Jen Kiggans, Virginia Republican, Faces Uproar Over Radio Host’s Racist Remark About Jeffries
item.description: Representative Jen Kiggans, a Republican in a tough re-election fight, faced criticism after she agreed with comments by a local radio host that included a racist line about Hakeem Jeffries, the Democratic minority leader.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Jen Kiggans of Virginia, a Republican, said on Monday night that her remarks had been misconstrued by Democrats.

item.title: China Sought Access to Anthropic’s Newest A.I. The Answer Was No.
item.description: The latest artificial intelligence models from Anthropic and OpenAI are extending the United States’ lead over China and intensifying the rivalry between the countries.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz, Julian E. Barnes, Sheera Frenkel and Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Marissa Leshnov for The New York Times
item.media:description: A growing number of national security officials and analysts have begun to liken the intensifying competition between China and the United States over artificial intelligence to the Cold War’s nuclear arms race.

item.title: Inflation Jumps as War-Driven Energy Costs Drive Up Prices
item.description: President Trump has declared the cease-fire on “life support,” and investors are taking a cautious approach as the economic effects of the war become more apparent.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz on Friday. Iran has effectively shut down the narrow waterway.

item.title: What to Watch in Tuesday’s Primaries in Nebraska and West Virginia
item.description: Democrats will choose a candidate in a House battleground in Nebraska that could decide the fate of Omaha’s “blue dot” vote.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: Some people are concerned that a quirk in how Nebraska allocates its Electoral College votes, known as the “blue dot" system, could be abolished by Republicans.
